Now I have a complete understanding of the system. Let me also check if Tandem browser is available, and look at what the study page currently shows vs what's needed.

The user wants to "teach the coach" - meaning they want to use the Tandem browser to show the Coach agent what the real GTO Wizard training interface looks like, so the Coach can understand the gap and add the right tasks to AGENTS.md.

But first, let me look at what the reference UI looks like - I know from the screenshot they attached what the post-flop training interface should show. The question is how to teach the coach.

The key insight from the coach-agent skill is:
- The Coach reviews Player commits against AGENTS.md success criteria
- The Coach also performs a **Backlog Health Check** at the end of each review cycle
- During backlog generation, the Coach investigates what's broken → scores gaps → generates tasks
- The Coach has a procedure for investigating the live site, but doesn't have a reference target to compare against

"Teaching the coach" means I need to:
1. Show the coach what the target interface looks like (using Tandem browser on the real GTO Wizard site if possible, or my analysis of the screenshot)
2. Update the AGENTS.md with the right task for building the interactive post-flop training
3. Maybe also update the coach-agent skill to include visual comparison as part of its investigation procedure

But actually, the user said "use the tandem browser to teach coach" - so let me check if Tandem browser is available. This might refer to the `browserbase` or `browser-trace` or `tandem-browser-shared-session` skill.

Let me check the tandem browser skill.

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "tandem-browser-shared-session", "description": "Fix Tandem Browser so the AI can see and share the same browsing session as the user", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: tandem-browser-shared-session
description: Fix Tandem Browser so the AI can see and share the same browsing session as the user
---

# Tandem Browser — Shared Session Setup

The Tandem Browser Electron app (user's GUI window) and the AI-accessible shared browser use **separate browser instances** with different user data dirs, so they don't share cookies/sessions.

Fix: add `--remote-debugging-port=9222` to the Electron app and connect a viewer to its **webview** CDP targets instead of a separate Playwright Chrome.

## Scripts

### `~/.hermes/scripts/start-tandem.sh`
Starts Tandem with remote debugging + viewer. Safe to re-run (kills old ports first).

### `~/.hermes/scripts/electron-viewer.js`
Node.js HTTP server that connects to the Electron CDP's **webview targets** (user's tabs, type `webview`) and serves a screenshot viewer at `localhost:3099`. Unlike `shared-browser.mjs`, it does NOT navigate on connect — only observes.

**Known limitations (CDP Runtime.evaluate):**
- The response structure for `Runtime.evaluate` with `returnByValue: true` is `{"id": N, "result": {"result": {"type": "...", "value": ACTUAL_VALUE}}}` — the actual data is at `m.result.result.value`, NOT `m.result.value`.
- Cross-origin iframes (like Google GSI sign-in buttons from `accounts.google.com/gsi/button`) cannot be clicked via `Runtime.evaluate` JS execution — their content is in a separate security context. Must use `Input.dispatchMouseEvent` with page coordinates (click at the iframe's bounding rect center).
- `Runtime.evaluate` on webview targets may time out if the page is mid-navigation or the webview has a pending load event. Navigate first, wait 3-5s, then evaluate.

**The `/click` endpoint must be added manually** — the base electron-viewer.js only ships `/screenshot.png`, `/info`, and `/navigate`. To enable coordinate-based clicking, add this block inside the HTTP handler before the HTML fallback:
```javascript
if (req.url === '/click' && req.method === 'POST') {
  let body = '';
  for await (const chunk of req) body += chunk;
  const { x, y } = JSON.parse(body);
  await cdp.send('Input.dispatchMouseEvent', { type: 'mousePressed', x, y, button: 'left', clickCount: 1 });
  await cdp.send('Input.dispatchMouseEvent', { type: 'mouseReleased', x, y, button: 'left', clickCount: 1 });
  res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' });
  res.end(JSON.stringify({ ok: true }));
  return;
}
```

**Dependency:** The script uses `require('ws')` — `ws` must be installed in the same directory: `cd /home/sc/.hermes/scripts && npm install ws`. Run this once; after install, the viewer can be started from any directory.

## New `/fill` and `/click-text` Endpoints

The `electron-viewer.js` has been extended with two HTTP endpoints for DOM-based form automation:

### `POST /fill` — Fill a form field by CSS selector

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/fill \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"selector": "[name=\"mobile\"]", "value": "0434968983"}'
```

```bash
# For <select> elements:
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/fill \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"selector": "select[name=\"country\"]", "value": "string:AU"}'
```

The endpoint sets `el.value`, dispatches `input` + `change` events, and calls `angular.element(el).triggerHandler()` for Angular awareness.

### `POST /click-text` — Click button/link by text content

```bash
# Exact match:
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/click-text \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"text": "Continue"}'

# Fuzzy match (contains):
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/click-text \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"text": "Australian cit"}'
```

The endpoint queries `button, a, input[type="submit"], [role="button"]` elements, tries exact text match first, then fuzzy contains match. Scrolls into view before clicking.

### Restart after adding endpoints

```bash
kill $(lsof -ti :3099) 2>/dev/null
sleep 1
node /home/sc/.hermes/scripts/electron-viewer.js > /tmp/electron-viewer.log 2>&1 &
sleep 2
cat /tmp/electron-viewer.log  # Verify "Connected"
```

## Application Form Automation

### AngularJS portals (Elmo, etc.)
Many employer career portals (NGS Super, others using Elmo talent systems) are AngularJS SPAs. DOM value changes via `el.value = '...'` do NOT propagate to the Angular model. Three approaches, in order of reliability:

1. **ngModel controller access** (most reliable for text inputs/selects):
   ```javascript
   const ngEl = angular.element(document.querySelector('[name="fieldName"]'));
   const ngModel = ngEl.controller('ngModel');
   ngModel.$setViewValue('desired-value');
   ngModel.$render();
   // For selects with prefixed values (e.g., "string:AU"), use the full option value
   ```

2. **Scope assignment** (for Angular model-backed forms):
   ```javascript
   const scope = angular.element(document.querySelector('[ng-app]')).scope();
   scope.$apply(function() {
     scope.data.contact.mobile = 'value';
   });
   ```

3. **DOM event dispatch** (least reliable — may not trigger Angular digest):
   ```javascript
   el.value = 'value';
   el.dispatchEvent(new Event('input', {bubbles: true}));
   el.dispatchEvent(new Event('change', {bubbles: true}));
   ```

4. **CDP `DOM.setFileInputFiles`** for file uploads — the only reliable way to set file inputs in AngularJS forms:
   ```javascript
   // Get document root
   const doc = await send('DOM.getDocument');
   const query = await send('DOM.querySelector', {
     nodeId: doc.result.root.nodeId,
     selector: '#resumeFile'
   });
   await send('DOM.setFileInputFiles', {
     nodeId: query.result.nodeId,
     files: ['/absolute/path/to/file.pdf']
   });
   ```

**Pitfalls with AngularJS forms:**
- Country selects often use prefixed values like `"string:AU"` instead of plain `"AU"`. Always inspect `select.options` to find the correct value.
- Radio buttons need `element.click()` + `element.checked = true` to satisfy Angular's two-way binding.
- After filling, click "Save" before "Next" — the save validates via Angular and persists. If "Next" is clicked before save, Angular shows validation errors even if values appear set.
- The `$scope` may not be directly accessible from the document body if the app uses component-based architecture. Always try `ngModel.$setViewValue` first.
- Multi-step forms update `window.location.hash` (e.g., `#/step2`). Monitor the hash to detect progress transitions.

### React SPAs (SEEK Quick Apply)

SEEK's Quick Apply uses React with a mix of `<select>` dropdowns and radio/checkbox inputs. Unlike Angular, React doesn't auto-detect DOM value changes — you must dispatch proper native events.

#### Select dropdowns (first 3 screening questions)

```javascript
const sel = document.querySelector('select[name="questionnaire.AU_Q_6_V_10"]');
sel.value = 'AU_Q_6_V_10_A_14970';  // Australian citizen
sel.dispatchEvent(new Event('change', {bubbles: true}));
```

Use the `/fill` endpoint for this — it dispatches `change` automatically:
```bash
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/fill \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"selector": "select[name=\"questionnaire.AU_Q_6_V_10\"]", "value": "AU_Q_6_V_10_A_14970"}'
```

#### Radio buttons (binary questions)

```javascript
const radio = document.querySelector('input[type="radio"][name="questionnaire.AU_Q_28763_V_3"][value="AU_Q_28763_V_3_A_28765"]');
radio.click();
radio.dispatchEvent(new Event('change', {bubbles: true}));
```

#### Checkboxes (multi-select questions)

```javascript
const cb = document.querySelector('input[type="checkbox"][name="questionnaire.AU_Q_28759_V_3"][value="..."]');
cb.click();
cb.dispatchEvent(new Event('change', {bubbles: true}));
```

#### File upload

React file inputs work the same as Angular — use `DOM.setFileInputFiles`:

```javascript
const doc = await send('DOM.getDocument');
const query = await send('DOM.querySelector', {
  nodeId: doc.result.root.nodeId,
  selector: '#resume-fileFile'
});
await send('DOM.setFileInputFiles', {
  nodeId: query.result.nodeId,
  files: ['/absolute/path/to/cv.pdf']
});
```

**Note:** The file input MUST be visible in the DOM. On SEEK, click the "Upload a resumé" radio first to reveal the file input.

#### SEEK application flow (multi-step)

```
1. /job/{id}/apply       → Choose documents (upload CV, set cover letter)
2. /job/{id}/apply/role-requirements  → Employer screening questions
3. /job/{id}/apply/profile           → Confirm SEEK profile
4. /job/{id}/apply/review            → Review & Submit
5. /job/{id}/apply/success           → Confirmation
```

**Check progress** via URL: the path segment after `/apply/` tells you which step.

#### Pitfalls with React forms (SEEK)

- Select dropdowns may have empty-string default values. Always inspect option values before setting.
- Radio and checkbox `value` attributes are opaque IDs, not human-readable text. Map labels to values in advance.
- The "Please make a selection" error overlay appears on validation failure. Check `document.querySelectorAll('[class*="error"], [class*="alert"]')` after attempting submit.
- SEEK profile step may have pre-filled data — just click Continue if no changes needed.
- The "Submit application" button text may include a non-breaking space: `"⁠Submit application"`.

## Checking Status

```bash
# Is Electron CDP accessible?
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9222/json/version

# List all page/webview targets
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9222/json | python3 -c "
import sys,json
for t in json.load(sys.stdin):
    print(f'  [{t[\"type\"]:8s}] {t.get(\"title\",\"?\")[:60]:60s} {t.get(\"url\",\"?\")}')"

# Get viewer info (what the AI sees)
curl -s http://localhost:3099/info
```

## Viewing & Controlling

The viewer at `localhost:3099` shows a screenshot of the webview it's connected to:

```bash
# Take a screenshot
curl -s -o /tmp/snap.png http://localhost:3099/screenshot.png

# Navigate the webview (opens in the user's Tandem tab)
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/navigate -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"url":"https://example.com"}'

# Click at coordinates
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/click -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"x": 400, "y": 300}'

# Type text
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3099/type -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"text":"hello world"}'
```

## Connecting to a Different Webview Target

If the user opens a new tab, the viewer may still be connected to the old webview. To switch:

1. List targets: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9222/json`
2. Find the new webview target ID
3. Restart the viewer (it auto-connects to the first webview)

## Verification After Submission

Always verify the outcome after submitting a form:

### Check the success page

The URL tells you the result:
- `/apply/success` on SEEK = submitted ✅
- Still on `/apply/review` or same step = validation failed or click didn't register

### Verify which documents were attached

Before clicking the final Submit, check the review page text for document names:

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:3099/info
# Then CDP to extract text
```

On SEEK's review page, look for:
- `"Resume"` + filename — confirms what was attached
- `"Cover letter"` + "No cover letter" / filename

**Critical SEEK pitfall:** SEEK's Quick Apply may use a **pre-existing resume from the user's SEEK profile** instead of the one you just uploaded. The evidence:
- The file upload shows `"CV - SRE.pdf"` in the upload field (progress indicator)
- But after the upload completes, the review page may show `"8/6/26 - Sean Cheong Software Engineer.pdf"` — an older resume that was already stored on the profile

**Mitigation:** After clicking "Upload a resumé" and using `DOM.setFileInputFiles`, navigate to the review step and verify which filename appears before clicking Submit. Use CDP to read the review page text and confirm the correct filename.

### Verify from the success page

```javascript
// After submit, check the page text
const text = await send('Runtime.evaluate', {
  expression: `document.body.innerText`,
  returnByValue: true
});
// Look for "Your application has been sent to" or "Thank you for applying"
const url = await send('Runtime.evaluate', {
  expression: `window.location.href`,
  returnByValue: true
});
// Confirmation URL patterns:
// SEEK  → /apply/success
// Elmo  → page with "Thank you for applying!" text
```

## User Preference: Show CV Screenshots Before Submitting

Before submitting a job application, always:
1. Generate the CV as a PDF
2. Take a screenshot via `Page.captureScreenshot` or Playwright
3. Present the screenshot to the user via `MEDIA:/path/to/screenshot.png`
4. Wait for explicit approval before clicking Submit

Do NOT skip this for any application — even if the same CV was used before. Each submission is a separate action that warrants verification.

## Pitfalls

- **`shared-browser.mjs` is destructive** — it navigates the first target it finds, hijacking the user's main window. Never use it.
- **Electron 40 requires glibc 2.35+** — won't run on Ubuntu 20.04. Error: `/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: version 'GLIBC_2.33' not found`. Root cause: native modules need C++20 compilation (GCC 11+), which ships with glibc 2.35+. Cannot work around — even source compilation fails because GCC 9/10 lack `<compare>`/`<source_location>` headers. Fix: upgrade to Ubuntu 22.04+ or run Tandem in Docker with X11 forwarding. Fallback: Chrome CDP viewer pattern.
- **`Target.createTarget` is not supported** in Electron's Chromium — you cannot create new hidden pages via CDP. Must connect to existing webview targets.
- **Port conflicts** — `EADDRINUSE :3099` means old viewer process is still running. Kill it first: `lsof -ti :3099 | xargs -r kill`
- **.mjs vs .js** — Electron-viewer uses `require('ws')` so must be `.js` (CommonJS), not `.mjs` (ESM), and needs `NODE_PATH` to find the `ws` module from tandem-browser's node_modules.
- **Module not found: ws** — Always run the viewer with `NODE_PATH=/home/sc/repos/tandem-browser/node_modules` or from the tandem-browser directory. Alternatively, install `ws` in the Hermes scripts dir: `cd /home/sc/.hermes/scripts && npm install ws`.
- **Viewer HTML has a click handler that POSTs to `/click` but the server may not have one** — The base `electron-viewer.js` only serves `/screenshot.png`, `/info`, and `/navigate`. If you try to click from the viewer UI, it'll silently return the HTML page instead. Add the `/click` endpoint as described above. Same applies to the `/type` endpoint for text input.
- **GSI iframes (Google Sign-In) require coordinate-based clicks** — SEEK, among other sites, uses Google's GSI flow where the sign-in button lives in a cross-origin iframe (`accounts.google.com/gsi/button`). JavaScript clicks via `Runtime.evaluate` cannot reach cross-origin iframe content. Use `Input.dispatchMouseEvent` with page coordinates instead. Find the iframe's position via the parent page's DOM: `document.querySelector('iframe[src*=\"accounts.google.com/gsi\"]').getBoundingClientRect()`, then click at the center.

messages.name: vision_analyze
messages.content:
{
  "success": true,
  "analysis": "This is a **poker strategy training interface** (likely a GTO solver or study tool) designed to help users analyze optimal play across different table positions, hands, and actions. Below is a full breakdown of its sections, interactive elements, and user actions:


### **1. Top Navigation Bar (Global Controls)**  
- **Leftmost Green "W" Logo**: Brand identifier (e.g., WPT or a poker strategy tool). Clickable to return to the app’s home or main menu.  
- **Game Type Buttons**:  
  - `Hold'em`, `PLO` (Pot-Limit Omaha), `Stud` (marked with a green "NEW" badge, indicating a recent feature), `Razz`, `Badugi`, `Play`: Click to switch between poker game variants.  
- **Mode Buttons**:  
  - `Study` (green background, brain icon): Active mode; focuses on strategy learning.  
  - `Practice`: Switch to simulated hand practice (no study tools).  
  - `Analyze`: Switch to post-hand or data analysis mode.  
- **Upgrade Button (Partial, Top Right)**: Triggers a subscription upgrade flow (for premium features).


### **2. Position Selector Row (Below Top Nav)**  
Displays table positions with stack sizes (e.g., `100` = 100 big blinds):  
- `GTO` (green dot: active): Game Theory Optimal mode (balanced, non-exploitative strategy).  
- `UTG 100` (green highlight, "Take action" below): Current selected position (Under the Gun, 100 BB stack).  
- `HJ 100`, `CO 100`, `BTN 100`, `SB 99.5`, `BB 99`: Click to switch to other positions (Hijack, Cutoff, Button, Small Blind, Big Blind) and adjust stack sizes.  


### **3. Main Content Split (Left vs. Right Columns)**  
The interface is divided into two columns for side-by-side analysis.


#### **Left Column: Hand Strategy Matrix**  
- **Tabs (Top of Left Column)**:  
  - `Strategy` (active, green underline): Displays hand ranges and action frequencies.  
  - `Ranges`: Focus on specific hand ranges (e.g., premium hands, all pairs).  
  - `Breakdown`: Break down hand performance by categories (e.g., suited vs. offsuit).  
  - `Reports: Flops` (1/100 with arrows): Paginated flop reports (navigable via left/right arrows).  

- **Hand Matrix**:  
  A standard poker hand chart where:  
  - Rows = Starting hands (e.g., `AA`, `AKo`, `AQo`—`o` = offsuit).  
  - Columns = Second card (e.g., `AKs`, `AQs`—`s` = suited).  
  - **Red-highlighted cells**: Premium hands (e.g., `AA`, `AKs`, `AQs`)—interactive: Click any cell to view detailed combo info in the right column.  


#### **Right Column: Action & Details**  
- **Top Tabs (Right Column)**:  
  - `Overview` (active): High-level strategy for the selected position.  
  - `Table`: Table-specific layout (e.g., opponent positions, stacks).  
  - `Equity chart`: Visualize hand equity against ranges.  

- **Position Buttons (Repeated)**:  
  `UTG 100` (active), `HJ 100`, `CO 100`, `BTN 100`, `SB 99.5`, `BB 99`: Click to switch positions (syncs with the top row).  

- **Contextual Data**:  
  - `2.5 BB`: Current bet size (2.5 big blinds).  
  - `Pot odds: 40%`: Odds of winning the pot (critical for decision-making).  

- **Actions Section**:  
  - `Actions` (dropdown arrow): Expand/collapse action filters (e.g., "Raise all sizes").  
  - **Action Buttons**:  
    - `Raise 2.5` (red): Active (15.4% frequency, 204 hand combos). Click to see details of raising 2.5 BB.  
    - `Call` (blue): Inactive (0.0% frequency, 0 combos) — no calls recommended here.  
    - `Fold` (green): Most common (84.6% frequency, 1122 combos).  

- **Detail Tabs (Bottom of Right Column)**:  
  - `Summary`: Quick recap of position strategy.  
  - `Filters`: Adjust hand ranges (e.g., exclude weak hands).  
  - `Blockers`: Analyze how opponent hands block your draws/hands.  
  - `Hands` (active): View combo details for the selected hand (e.g., how many `AA` combos exist in UTG 100).  

- **Combo Details Text Box**:  
  "Click a hand in the matrix to see combo details" — dynamic area updating when a hand is selected.


### **User Actions (Key Interactions)**  
Users can:  
1. Switch between poker games (Hold’em → PLO → Stud, etc.).  
2. Toggle modes (Study → Practice → Analyze).  
3. Upgrade to a premium subscription.  
4. Select table positions (UTG, HJ, etc.) to analyze strategy for each spot.  
5. Navigate through flop reports (1/100) to study different board textures.  
6. Click any hand in the matrix (e.g., `AKo`) to view its combo frequency, action split, and context-specific details.  
7. Switch between Overview/Table/Equity Chart views to visualize data differently.  
8. Explore detail tabs (Filters, Blockers) to refine or deepen analysis.  
9. Study action frequencies (e.g., "Fold 84.6% in UTG 100") to learn optimal GTO decisions.


This interface is built for serious poker players to master GTO strategy, with every element designed to drill down into position-specific, hand-specific, and action-specific insights."
}

{

  "analysis": "This image depicts a **login page for "GTO Wizard"** (likely a poker/GTO analysis tool, given the name). Here’s a detailed breakdown:  


### 1. Visual Layout & Elements  
- **Background**: Dark (black/near-black) to make elements stand out.  
- **Top-Right Icon**: A small UK flag (Union Jack) suggests language/region settings (English/UK).  
- **Logo**: A stylized green “W” (with a lightning bolt-like design inside) sits at the top center, representing the GTO Wizard brand.  
- **Heading**: Below the logo, white text reads *“Login to GTO Wizard”* (clearly indicating the page’s purpose: authentication).  


### 2. Login Options (Social Authentication)  
Three horizontal buttons offer login via major platforms:  
- **Google Button**: Red background with the multicolored Google “G” logo and white text *“GOOGLE”*.  
- **Facebook Button**: Blue background with the white Facebook “f” logo and white text *“FACEBOOK”*.  
- **Apple Button**: Black background with the white Apple logo and white text *“APPLE”*.  


### 3. Navigation & Consent  
- **Homepage Link**: Below the login buttons, a white text link reads *“< GO TO HOMEPAGE”* (a back button to return to the site’s homepage).  
- **Cookie Consent Banner (Bottom)**:  
  - **Text (Left)**: Explains cookie usage: *“We use cookies to get a better read on how you navigate our site, helping us to continue building the most innovative tools to improve your skills. Your data is in safe hands—we’re all-in on protecting your privacy.”*  
  - **Link**: *“About Cookies”* (white text, likely leads to more details).  
  - **Buttons (Right)**:  
    - *“Allow All Cookies”* (teal background, white text) — accepts all cookies.  
    - *“Reject Non-Essential Cookies”* (black background, white text) — rejects non-essential cookies (essential cookies remain).  


### 4. Overall Purpose  
This is a **login interface** for GTO Wizard, prioritizing social authentication (Google, Facebook, Apple) for convenience. The dark theme, clear branding, and cookie consent (to comply with privacy regulations like GDPR) suggest a modern, user-friendly design focused on accessibility and transparency.  


In summary: The page is a login screen for GTO Wizard, offering social login options, a homepage link, and a cookie consent banner explaining data usage."
}

messages.content:
{"content": "1|"""
2|gRPC client for the GTO Solver service.
3|Direct connection to the gRPC solver server (bypasses Celery).
4|"""
5|import os
6|import logging
7|import grpc
8|from typing import Optional
9|
10|logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
11|
12|SOLVER_GRPC_HOST = os.environ.get("SOLVER_GRPC_HOST", "localhost")
13|SOLVER_GRPC_PORT = int(os.environ.get("SOLVER_GRPC_PORT", "50051"))
14|
15|# Add proto directory to path at module level
16|import sys
17|_proto_dir = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)),
18|                           "..", "..", "..", "apps", "solver", "proto")
19|if _proto_dir not in sys.path:
20|    sys.path.insert(0, _proto_dir)
21|
22|import solver_pb2_grpc
23|import solver_pb2
24|
25|# Lazy import to avoid circular deps
26|_client = None
27|
28|def get_solver_client():
29|    """Get or create a gRPC channel to the solver server."""
30|    global _client
31|    if _client is None:
32|        try:
33|            import sys
34|            _proto_dir = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), 
35|                                       *['..', '..', '..', 'apps', 'solver', 'proto'])
36|            if _proto_dir not in sys.path:
37|                sys.path.insert(0, _proto_dir)
38|            import solver_pb2_grpc
39|            import solver_pb2
40|
41|            target = f"{SOLVER_GRPC_HOST}:{SOLVER_GRPC_PORT}"
42|            channel = grpc.insecure_channel(target, options=[
43|                ('grpc.connect_timeout_ms', 5000),
44|                ('grpc.max_send_message_length', 50 * 1024 * 1024),
45|                ('grpc.max_receive_message_length', 50 * 1024 * 1024),
46|            ])
47|            _client = solver_pb2_grpc.SolverServiceStub(channel)
48|            logger.info(f"Solver gRPC client connected to {target}")
49|        except Exception as e:
50|            logger.error(f"Failed to create solver gRPC client: {e}")
51|            return None
52|    return _client
53|
54|
55|def submit_solve(
56|    game_type: str = "nlh",
57|    players: int = 2,
58|    board: Optional[str] = None,
59|    pot_size: int = 100,
60|    stack_depth: int = 100,
61|    bet_sizes: Optional[list] = None,
62|    iterations: int = 1000,
63|    street: str = "river",
64|    position: str = "BTN",
65|) -> dict:
66|    """Submit a solve job to the gRPC solver server."""
67|    import solver_pb2 as pb2
68|    client = get_solver_client()
69|    if client is None:
70|        return {
71|            "status": "error",
72|            "progress": 0,
73|            "strategy": [],
74|            "error": "gRPC solver client not available"
75|        }
76|
77|    try:
78|        req = pb2.SolveRequest(
79|            game_type=game_type,
80|            players=players,
81|            board=board or "",
82|            pot_size=pot_size,
83|            stack_depth=stack_depth,
84|            bet_sizes=bet_sizes or [],
85|            iterations=iterations,
86|            street=street,
87|            position=position,
88|        )
89|        resp = client.SubmitSolve(req, timeout=30)
90|        job_id = resp.job_id
91|
92|        # Poll until complete (sync solve runs in background thread)
93|        import time
94|        max_wait = 30  # seconds
95|        poll_interval = 0.5
96|        waited = 0
97|        status_resp = resp
98|        while waited < max_wait:
99|            if status_resp.status == "complete":
100|                break
101|            time.sleep(poll_interval)
102|            waited += poll_interval
103|            try:
104|                status_req = pb2.SolveRequest(job_id=job_id)
105|                status_resp = client.GetSolveStatus(status_req, timeout=10)
106|            except Exception:
107|                pass
108|
109|        return {
110|            "job_id": resp.job_id,
111|            "status": resp.status,
112|            "progress": resp.progress,
113|            "strategy": [
114|                {
115|                    "action": a.action,
116|                    "frequency": a.frequency,
117|                    "ev": a.ev,
118|                }
119|                for a in resp.strategy
120|            ],
121|            "strategy_key": resp.strategy_key,
122|            "error": resp.error or None,
123|        }
124|    except Exception as e:
125|        logger.error(f"Solver gRPC call failed: {e}")
126|        return {
127|            "status": "error",
128|            "progress": 0,
129|            "strategy": [],
130|            "error": str(e),
131|        }
132|
133|
134|def check_health() -> dict:
135|    """Check solver server health."""
136|    try:
137|        import solver_pb2 as pb2
138|        client = get_solver_client()
139|        if client is None:
140|            return {"status": "unavailable", "detail": "gRPC client not created"}
141|        req = pb2.HealthRequest()
142|        resp = client.HealthCheck(req, timeout=5)
143|        return {"status": resp.status, "detail": getattr(resp, 'detail', '')}
144|    except Exception as e:
145|        return {"status": "unreachable", "detail": str(e)}

{"content": "1|"""
2|Solver API Router — GTO solve workflows.
3|
4|Direct integration with the MCCFR engine (bypasses gRPC/Celery).
5|Supports preflop range solving for the study page.
6|"""
7|import sys, os, json, logging
8|from pathlib import Path
9|
10|# Add paths for solver engine access
11|_here = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
12|_solver_dir = os.path.join(_here, "..", "..", "..", "apps", "solver")
13|_poker_dir = os.path.join(_here, "..", "..", "..", "packages", "poker-core", "src")
14|for p in [_solver_dir, _poker_dir]:
15|    if p not in sys.path:
16|        sys.path.insert(0, p)
17|
18|from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
19|from pydantic import BaseModel
20|from typing import Optional, List
21|
22|logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
23|router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/v1/solver", tags=["solver"])
24|
25|# ── Engine availability cache ──
26|_engine_available = None
27|
28|def _check_engine():
29|    global _engine_available
30|    if _engine_available is None:
31|        try:
32|            from cfr.engine import CFREngine
33|            _engine_available = True
34|        except ImportError:
35|            _engine_available = False
36|    return _engine_available
37|
38|# ── Precomputed chart cache ──
39|_charts_dir = Path(_solver_dir) / "strategy" / "charts"
40|_chart_cache = {}
41|
42|def _load_chart(position: str, stack_depth: int) -> dict | None:
43|    """Load push/fold chart for a given position and stack depth."""
44|    # Find nearest available depth
45|    depths = sorted([int(f.stem.split("_")[1].replace("bb", ""))
46|                     for f in _charts_dir.glob("push_*bb_*.json")])
47|    if not depths:
48|        return None
49|    nearest = min(depths, key=lambda d: abs(d - stack_depth))
50|    key = f"push_{nearest}bb_{position}"
51|    if key not in _chart_cache:
52|        path = _charts_dir / f"{key}.json"
53|        if path.exists():
54|            with open(path) as f:
55|                _chart_cache[key] = json.load(f)
56|        else:
57|            return None
58|    return _chart_cache[key]

118|async def solve(req: SolveRequest):
119|    """
120|    Solve a GTO spot using the direct solver path.
121|    Supports river spots with a defined board.
122|    """
123|    try:
124|        if not _check_engine():
125|            return SolveResponse(
126|                status="error", progress=0,
127|                error="Solver engine not available",
128|                message="Install phevaluator and rebuild",
129|            )
130|
131|        from cfr.engine import CFREngine
132|        from games.texas_hold_em import TexasHoldEm, create_river_state, ActionType
133|        from gto_poker.deck import Deck
134|        from gto_poker.hand import HandEvaluator
135|
136|        evaluator = HandEvaluator()
137|        game = TexasHoldEm()
138|        engine = CFREngine(game=game, seed=42)
139|
140|        board_strings = []
141|        if req.board and len(req.board) >= 6:
142|            board_strings = [req.board[i:i+2] for i in range(0, len(req.board), 2)]
143|
144|        if req.street == "river" and len(board_strings) >= 3:
145|            state = create_river_state(
146|                p0_cards=["Ah", "Kh"],
147|                p1_cards=["Kc", "Qc"],
148|                board=board_strings[:3],
149|                pot=req.pot_size,
150|                stacks=[req.stack_depth, req.stack_depth],
151|            )
152|            strategies = engine.solve(
153|                initial_state=state,
154|                iterations=min(req.iterations, 500),
155|            )
156|        else:
157|            strategies = {}
158|
159|        actions = []
160|        for key, avg_strat in strategies.items():
161|            info = engine.infoset_manager.get(key)
162|            if info is not None:
163|                for i, act in enumerate(info.actions):
164|                    freq = float(avg_strat[i]) if i < len(avg_strat) else 0.0
165|                    if freq > 0.01:
166|                        actions.append(StrategyAction(
167|                            action=str(act),
168|                            frequency=round(freq, 4),
169|                            ev=0.0,
170|                        ))
171|
172|        return SolveResponse(
173|            status="complete",
174|            progress=100,
175|            strategy=actions,
176|            message=f"Solved {req.street} spot ({len(strategies)} infosets)",
177|        )
178|
179|    except ImportError as e:

181|        return SolveResponse(

183|            error=str(e),

213|    },

218|        "call_actions": [],

220|    "SB": {

222|        "call_width": 0.0,
223|        "raise_actions": ["raise_3bb"],
224|        "call_actions": [],
225|    },
226|    "BB": {

231|    },

234|
235|def _generate_range(position: str, stack_depth: int) -> tuple[list[HandCell], str, bool]:
236|    """
237|    Generate preflop ranges using:
238|    1. Precomputed push/fold charts for short stacks (<40bb)
239|    2. GTO range model with real equities for deeper stacks
240|    """
241|    ranks = ['A', 'K', 'Q', 'J', 'T', '9', '8', '7', '6', '5', '4', '3', '2']
242|
243|    # Build all 169 hands
244|    hands_169 = []
245|    for i, r1 in enumerate(ranks):
246|        for j, r2 in enumerate(ranks):
247|            if i <= j:
248|                if r1 == r2:
249|                    hands_169.append(f"{r1}{r2}")
250|                else:
251|                    hands_169.append(f"{r1}{r2}s")
252|                    hands_169.append(f"{r1}{r2}o")
253|
254|    solver_available = _check_engine()
255|    source = ""
256|
257|    # ── Shallow stacks: use push/fold charts ──
258|    if stack_depth <= 60:
259|        chart = _load_chart(position, stack_depth)
260|        if chart:
261|            source = f"push-fold-chart-{stack_depth}bb"
262|            cells = []
263|            # Compute equities using HandEvaluator for display
264|            try:
265|                from gto_poker.hand import HandEvaluator
266|                evaluator = HandEvaluator()
267|            except ImportError:
268|                evaluator = None
269|
270|            for hand in hands_169:
271|                chart_action = chart.get(hand, "fold")
272|                action = "raise" if chart_action == "push" else "fold"
273|                equity = 0.5
274|                eq = _get_preflop_equity(hand)
275|                if eq is not None:
276|                    equity = round(eq, 4)
277|                cells.append(HandCell(
278|                    hand=hand,
279|                    action=action,
280|                    frequency=1.0 if action == "raise" else 0.0,
281|                    equity=equity,
282|                ))
283|            return cells, source, False
284|
285|    # ── Deep stacks: use equity-based GTO range model ──
286|    try:
287|        from gto_poker.hand import HandEvaluator
288|        evaluator = HandEvaluator()
289|    except ImportError:
290|        evaluator = None
291|
292|    # Get range config for this position
293|    config = _PREFLOP_RANGES.get(position, _PREFLOP_RANGES["UTG"])
294|
295|    # Compute equity for each hand using precomputed data
296|    hand_equities = []
297|    for hand in hands_169:
298|        eq = _get_preflop_equity(hand)
299|        hand_equities.append((hand, eq if eq is not None else 0.5))
300|
301|    # Sort by equity descending
302|    hand_equities.sort(key=lambda x: -x[1])
303|
304|    # Assign actions: top `width`% raise, next `call_width`% call (if any), rest fold
305|    total = len(hand_equities)  # 169
306|    raise_count = int(total * config["width"])
307|    call_count = int(total * config.get("call_width", 0))
308|    fold_count = total - raise_count - call_count
309|
310|    action_map = {}
311|    for i, (hand, eq) in enumerate(hand_equities):
312|        if i < raise_count:
313|            # Raise — with frequency tapering at the bottom of the range
314|            position_in_range = i / max(raise_count, 1)
315|            freq = max(0.5, 1.0 - position_in_range * 0.5)
316|            action_map[hand] = (config["raise_actions"][0] if config["raise_actions"] else "raise", round(freq, 3), round(eq, 4))
317|        elif i < raise_count + call_count:
318|            action_map[hand] = (config["call_actions"][0] if config["call_actions"] else "call", 1.0, round(eq, 4))
319|        else:
320|            action_map[hand] = ("fold", 1.0, round(eq, 4))
321|
322|    # Build response in the original display order (matrix order)
323|    cells = []
324|    for hand in hands_169:
325|        action, freq, eq = action_map.get(hand, ("fold", 0.0, 0.5))
326|        cells.append(HandCell(hand=hand, action=action, frequency=freq, equity=eq))
327|
328|    source = "equity-model"
329|    if solver_available:
330|        source += "+mccfr"
331|    else:
332|        source += "+heuristic"
333|
334|    return cells, source, solver_available
335|
336|
337|# Load precomputed preflop equities
338|_preflop_equities = {}
339|_eq_cache_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(__file__)), "data", "preflop_equities.json")
340|if os.path.exists(_eq_cache_path):
341|    with open(_eq_cache_path) as f:
342|        _preflop_equities = json.load(f)
343|else:
344|    _preflop_equities = {}
345|
346|

348|    """Get precomputed preflop equity for a hand."""

{"content":"# AGENTS.md — GTO Wizard Clone

## About
Open-source GTO poker training platform. Equity calculator, CFR solver, training modes, hand history analysis, ICM calculator, push/fold charts, and training courses. Live at `wiz.codeovertcp.com`.

**Status:** Active development — core features exist, polish needed.

**Reference target:** See `docs/reference-study-interface.png` for the exact interactive training interface the study page should replicate.

## Architecture

### Stack
- **Frontend**: Next.js 15 + React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui
- **Backend**: Python 3.12 FastAPI on port 8002
- **Solver**: Python MCCFR engine (apps/solver/) with gRPC
- **Cache**: Redis (fakeredis fallback)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL (Neon) via SQLAlchemy
- **Monorepo**: Nx with TurboRepo

### Key Directories
| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `apps/web/` | Next.js frontend (22+ page routes) |
| `apps/web/src/app/` | Page routes by path |
| `apps/web/src/components/` | Shared React components (equity, stud, badugi, hh, icm, train) |
| `apps/web/src/lib/` | API client library |
| `apps/api/` | FastAPI backend (11 routers) |
| `apps/api/routers/` | API route handlers |
| `apps/solver/` | MCCFR solver engine |
| `packages/poker-core/` | Shared poker logic (equity, hand eval, range, variants) |
| `packages/ui-components/` | Shared UI components |
| `packages/types/` | TypeScript type definitions |

### Available API Routes
`/api/v1/equity`, `/api/v1/variants`, `/api/v1/solver`, `/api/v1/courses`, `/api/v1/quiz`,
`/api/v1/icm`, `/api/v1/hh`, `/api/v1/strategy`, `/api/v1/plo4`, `/api/v1/omaha`,
`/api/v1/double-board`, `/api/v1/bomb-pot`

### Solver API (for post-flop training)
`POST /api/v1/solver/solve` accepts `board`, `street`, `pot_size`, `stack_depth`, `bet_sizes`, `position` and returns GTO strategy actions with frequencies and EV. This is the backend that powers the interactive study page training mode. Currently supports river solving with a defined board.

## Conventions
- **Setup**: `source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -e packages/poker-core`
- **Backend dev**: `PYTHONPATH=apps/api python -m uvicorn main:app --reload --port 8002`
- **Frontend dev**: `cd apps/web && npm run dev`
- **Tests**: `uv sync` first (pytest not currently in venv), then `python -m pytest packages/poker-core/tests/`
- **Commits**: Conventional commits (`feat:`, `fix:`, `test:`, `docs:`)
- **Python**: 3.12+, ruff linting (line-length 100)

## Skills
These skills are loaded automatically when the Player works on this project:

| Skill | When | Why |
|-------|------|-----|
| `subagent-driven-development` | Complex features with multiple independent components | Parallel delegation for speed |
| `test-driven-development` | Test-heavy tasks (fix-dev-env, add-e2e-tests) | Enforce red-green-refactor discipline |
| `systematic-debugging` | Any task where tests fail or behavior is unexpected | 4-phase root cause debugging |

## Tasks

Ordered by priority. Each task is one unit of work for one player tick.

### Task: keep-api-server-running
- **Description**: The FastAPI backend on port 8000 crashes on container restart or host reboot. Set up a systemd --user service (or screen/tmux wrapper) that auto-restarts the API when it dies. The API runs from the repo root with `PYTHONPATH=apps/api uv run uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000`. Also install fakeredis (already done) and ensure `uv sync --group runtime` has all needed deps.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `curl http://localhost:8000/api/v1/health` returns 200 after the service starts
  - Service auto-restarts if the process crashes
  - Service starts on boot (systemd --user enable)
- **Coach checks**:
  - Verify the service file exists at ~/.config/systemd/user/gto-api.service
  - Test stop/start cycle: `systemctl --user stop gto-api && systemctl --user start gto-api` then curl health
  - Check journalctl for errors

### Task: fix-strategy-lookup
- **Description**: The `GET /api/v1/strategy-lookup` endpoint returns HTTP 500 with error `'dict' object has no attribute 'game_type'`. The root cause is that `get_strategy()` returns a `StoredStrategy` object, but when the strategy is not found in the database and the fallback path (`list_strategies`) is hit, the candidate iteration or subsequent handling produces a dict instead. Fix the type mismatch in `apps/api/routers/strategy_lookup.py` or `apps/api/services/strategy_storage.py` so strategy lookups work correctly.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `curl "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/strategy-lookup?board=preflop&stack_depth=100&position=UTG"` returns a valid JSON response (may be 404 if no seed data — that's acceptable) rather than a 500
  - Strategy lookup with valid parameters returns strategy data
- **Coach checks**:
  - The fix handles both found and not-found paths cleanly
  - No regression in other endpoint behaviour
  - The fix doesn't silently swallow errors

### Task: seed-preflop-strategies
- **Description**: The strategy_lookup endpoint returns 404 because no strategy data exists in the PostgreSQL database. Create a seed script that inserts the pre-computed preflop GTO ranges (UTG, HJ, CO, BTN, SB, BB for 100bb) from the solver output into the strategies table. The seed data should be generated by the solver or sourced from the existing preflop data embedded in the frontend.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `curl "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/strategy-lookup?board=preflop&stack_depth=100&position=UTG"` returns 200 with strategy data
  - All 6 common positions have preflop data
- **Coach checks**:
  - Seed script is idempotent (safe to run multiple times)
  - Strategy data matches expected GTO ranges
  - Seed script is documented in AGENTS.md

### Task: fix-solver-docker-build
- **Description**: `docker compose build solver` fails with a numpy version conflict (`numpy==2.2.3` vs `numba 0.61.0` requiring `numpy<2.2`). The solver Dockerfile at `apps/solver/Dockerfile` or its requirements.txt has pinned version conflicts. Fix the dependency versions so the solver image builds cleanly.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `docker compose build solver` exits 0
- **Coach checks**:
  - Check that the solver starts: `docker compose up -d solver` then check logs
  - Verify the solver gRPC port 50051 is listening

### Task: fix-solver-protobuf-version
- **Description**: The solver Docker image builds but crashes at runtime with `VersionError: Detected incompatible Protobuf Gencode/Runtime versions when loading solver.proto: gencode 6.31.1 runtime 5.29.6`. Pin `protobuf>=6.31.1` or re-pin grpcio-tools to match the runtime protobuf in `apps/solver/requirements.txt` so the solver service starts without crashing.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `docker compose up -d solver` starts without protobuf error
  - `docker logs gto-wizard-clone-solver-1` shows no VersionError
  - `curl http://localhost:8000/api/v1/solver/health` returns 200 or the solver gRPC port 50051 is listening
- **Coach checks**:
  - Verify protobuf version in requirements.txt is compatible with the compiled proto files
  - Verify solver health endpoint responds

### Task: document-seed-script
- **Description**: Add usage instructions for `apps/api/prisma/seed_preflop_strategies.py` to the AGENTS.md Conventions or Seed Data section. Ensure a newcomer can read AGENTS.md and know how to populate the strategy database.
- **Success criteria**:
  - AGENTS.md has explicit instructions for running the seed script
- **Coach checks**:
  - Verify the documentation exists in AGENTS.md
  - Verify the instructions are accurate (run the command and confirm it works)

### Task: automate-seed-on-deploy
- **Description**: Add a Makefile target or post-deploy hook (e.g., in docker-compose or a systemd service unit) that runs `seed_preflop_strategies.py` after database migrations to ensure preflop strategy data exists in all environments (local, preview, production).
- **Success criteria**:

  - Running the command twice is idempotent (same result)

The page should fetch a random spot, display the board/hand/options, let the user pick an action, and show the GTO answer with EV comparison.

### Task: postflop-solver-api-endpoint
- **Description**: Create a dedicated `POST /api/v1/solver/postflop-strategy` endpoint that the study page's interactive training mode will call. Unlike the existing `/solve` endpoint (which runs a live CFR solve — too slow for interactive use), this endpoint returns cached/quick pre-computed strategy data for common postflop spots. Accept `board` (string like "KsKc3s"), `position` (player acting), `street` (flop/turn/river), `pot_size`, `stack_depth`, `hero_hand`. Return top N actions (check, bet sizes, fold, call, raise) with GTO frequency % and EV. The `/solve` endpoint already supports postflop parameters as a fallback — if no cached data exists, fall through to call the live solver engine with a 30-second timeout.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/v1/solver/postflop-strategy -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"board":"KsKc3s","position":"BTN","street":"flop","pot_size":5.5,"stack_depth":97.5}'` returns 200 with action list

- **Description**: Add an interactive postflop training mode to the /study page. The page currently shows a static preflop range viewer. Add a new mode (toggled via a "Postflop Training" / "Preflop Ranges" switch) that shows:
  1. **Board cards** — 3-5 community card slots (flop/turn/river). Parse from a card string like "KsKc3s" and render styled cards with suit colors.
  2. **Pot size** — displays current pot, updates per street.
  3. **Active player highlight** — green-bordered column showing whose turn it is.
  4. **Action history** — for each position still in the hand, show whether they folded or their action so far.
  5. **Interactive action buttons** — user can click an action to make a decision. Buttons should be styled per type: CHECK, BET 33%/50%/75%/125%, FOLD, CALL, RAISE 50%/100%, ALLIN (all-in with remaining stack). Each button shows chip amount + pot %.
  6. **GTO comparison** — after user picks an action, overlay the GTO strategy from the API for each action option as micro-chips (frequency %, EV). Highlight the user's choice vs the GTO-recommended action.
  
  Reference the image at `docs/reference-study-interface.png` for exact layout and styling. The left column should show the strategy matrix (preflop view) or board+actions (postflop view). The right column should always show the GTO action breakdown and EV comparison.
- **Success criteria**:
  - `/study` page loads without console errors
  - Toggle between "Preflop Ranges" and "Postflop Training" modes works
  - Postflop mode shows: board cards, pot size, active player highlight, action history per position

  - Clicking an action button calls `POST /api/v1/solver/postflop-strategy` and shows GTO comparison feedback
  - Reference screenshot at `docs/reference-study-interface.png` matches the rendered UI layout
- **Coach checks**:

  - Verify the preflop/postflop toggle exists and switches views
  - Verify board cards render with correct suit symbols and colors
  - Click each action button type — verify API call happens and response renders
  - Compare rendered UI against `docs/reference-study-interface.png`: verify action button layout, bet sizing notation, pot size display, position column layout all match
  - Check mobile/small viewport doesn't break the layout entirely (responsive enough to not lose buttons)

### Task: postflop-spot-configuration
- **Description**: Add a configuration panel to the postflop training mode that lets the user set up a training scenario. Before starting a postflop hand, the user should be able to:
  1. Select which positions are in the hand (e.g., BTN vs BB heads-up, or multi-way)
  2. Input the board cards (3-5 community cards)
  3. Set pot size and stack depths
  4. Choose the street (flop/turn/river)
  5. Optionally input hero's hole cards to see personalized GTO strategy
  
  The configuration should be a collapsible panel that opens with a "Configure Spot" button and collapses after setup. Default configuration: BTN vs BB, K♠K♣3♠ flop, 5.5bb pot, 100bb stacks — matching the reference image at `docs/reference-study-interface.png`.
- **Success criteria**:
  - Configuration panel opens/closes with button toggle
  - User can select positions, enter board cards, set pot/stack
  - Changes take effect and the action buttons update
  - Default config matches the reference image
- **Coach checks**:
  - Open the config panel, change a setting, verify the board/pot/position updates
  - Close and re-open the config panel, verify settings persist
  - Verify the default config renders identically to `docs/reference-study-interface.png`

### Task: postflop-street-navigation
- **Description**: Add street-by-street navigation to the postflop training mode. After the user makes an action on one street (e.g., flop), they should be able to advance to the next street (turn, then river). Each street:
  1. Reveals new community cards
  2. Updates the pot size based on previous action
  3. Generates a new set of GTO action buttons for the active player
  
  Include a hand history breadcrumb at the top showing: Preflop actions → Flop (current) → Turn (locked) → River (locked) — with past streets showing what action was taken.
- **Success criteria**:
  - After selecting an action, user can advance to next street
  - New board cards appear on turn/river
  - Pot size updates correctly
  - New action buttons appear for the active player on each street
  - Street breadcrumb shows past actions per street
- **Coach checks**:
  - Advance through flop → turn → river, verify each street shows correct board + pot
  - Verify the street breadcrumb updates correctly
  - Verify the action buttons change per street (more options on river with all-in)

## Coach Configuration
- **Review scope**: git diff of latest commit, test output, success criteria from AGENTS.md task, console errors from frontend pages (check via curl/browser)
- **Pass conditions**: All success criteria for the task are met. No regression in previously passing tests. No console errors introduced.
- **Fail actions** (descending severity):
  1. Coach creates a corrective commit fixing the issue directly
  2. Coach reverts the commit and creates a fix task pinned to the issue
  3. For ambiguous or high-risk failures, coach blocks and tags for human review

**Reference for visual comparison**: The file `docs/reference-study-interface.png` is a screenshot of the target interactive postflop training interface. When reviewing tasks `interactive-postflop-study-buttons`, `postflop-spot-configuration`, and `postflop-street-navigation`, the coach SHOULD load this image via `vision_analyze` to compare the player's rendered output against the visual reference. Specific visual elements to match:
- Action button layout (BET sizes as 33%/50%/75%/125% of pot)
- Position column layout (UTG/HJ/CO/BTN/SB/BB in that order, with green highlight on active)
- Board card display (centered, with suit colors)
- Pot size notation (e.g., "5.5" next to "FLOP" label)
- Street breadcrumb style (tabs with active street highlighted)

## Seed Data
- **Run**: `cd /home/sc/repos/gto-wizard-clone && PYTHONPATH=apps/api .venv/bin/python apps/api/prisma/seed_preflop_strategies.py`

{"success": true, "name": "coach-agent", "description": "Autonomous development reviewer — the Coach in a coach/player loop. Reviews player commits against AGENTS.md success criteria, outputs DECISION: APPROVE/FIX/REVERT with evidence.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: coach-agent
description: "Autonomous development reviewer — the Coach in a coach/player loop. Reviews player commits against AGENTS.md success criteria, outputs DECISION: APPROVE/FIX/REVERT with evidence."
---

# coach-agent — Autonomous Development Reviewer

You are the **Coach** in a coach/player development loop. Your role is to **review** — validate the Player's commits against the original task criteria from AGENTS.md, enforce quality standards, and decide: APPROVE, FIX, or REVERT.

You are the adversarial agent. Your skepticism makes the system trustworthy. You verify every claim with evidence.

## Core Principle: Fresh Context

You run as a completely separate agent from the Player — no shared history, no accumulated context from the Player's session. You evaluate the Player's output as if you've never seen it before. This prevents rationalization of the Player's shortcuts. This is the single most important design decision — use this advantage.

## Model Separation (Aspirational — See Pitfall)

The ideal design calls for the Coach to run on a different model than the Player for independent reasoning. In practice:

- **Stable choice**: `deepseek-v4-flash` via `opencode-go` (same as Player) for reliability
- **Unstable choice**: `openrouter/owl-alpha` was tried for stronger reasoning but proved unreliable due to HTTP 429 rate limits on the free tier — the Coach failed to run for hours, leaving commits unreviewed

**Pitfall: Model reliability > Model separation.** A Coach that fails to run is worse than a Coach that shares the Player's model. If the Coach's cron errors with HTTP 429 or "Provider returned error", player commits go unreviewed. **Use the same reliable model as the Player** — the adversarial review protocol (evidence gates, approval sentinel, revert authority) provides independence, not the model choice.

The cron job `coach-development-loop` pins its model explicitly — update via `cronjob(action='update', job_id='5e1bba516d87', model={'provider': '<provider>', 'model': '<model>'})`.

## Rubble-Stamp Prevention

Five mechanisms prevent you from approving without genuine review:
1. **No batching**: Review one commit/unit at a time
2. **Minimum evidence requirement**: Each criterion needs explicit evidence (diff quote, curl output, test result)
3. **Approval sentinel**: Your verdict must start with `DECISION: APPROVE/FIX/REVERT` — no prose-only approvals
4. **Separation of duties**: You are a completely separate agent with no shared context
5. **Requirements anchoring**: Evaluate against AGENTS.md criteria only, NOT the player's commit message or self-report

## Review Protocol

### Step 1: Find What to Review
Read the master checkpoint at `/home/sc/.hermes/master-checkpoint.json` to find the last active project and its `last_run` timestamp. If the last run's project/task has already been reviewed (checkpoint shows `coach: "approved"` on the latest completed entry), report `[SILENT]`.

Also read the project's own `.checkpoint.json` — it may have been updated by the player since the last coach run. Compare `last_sha` to the project's `git log -1 --format=%H`. If they match and the latest completed entry has `coach: "approved"`, report `[SILENT]`.

### Step 2: Read the Original Criteria
Read the project's AGENTS.md. Determine which task the player was working on:

- **If the commit matches a completed[] entry**: Use that task's criteria.
- **If the commit doesn't match current_task or any completed[] entry**: The player jumped tasks. Identify the AGENTS.md task that best matches the commit's actual changes (by file paths, component names, etc.). Evaluate against THAT task's criteria, and note the task mismatch in your report. Do NOT skip the review just because the player changed tasks.
- **If the commit doesn't match ANY AGENTS.md task**: Evaluate against general quality standards (no broken tests, no unrelated files, code is functional).

Read the matched task's:
- **Success criteria** — what the Player claimed to achieve
- **Coach checks** — specific verification points you must validate

You evaluate against the AGENTS.md criteria, NOT the Player's commit message or self-report. The Player's description of what they did is irrelevant — evaluate what they actually produced.

### Step 3: Read the Diff
- `git show HEAD --stat` — what files changed in the latest commit (use this, NOT `git log -1 --stat` which can include working-tree changes)
- Alternatively: `git diff HEAD~1..HEAD --stat` for the same result
- `git show --format="" -- <files>` — the actual changes (only the files modified by the task)

**Pitfall**: `git log -1 --stat` shows working-tree-influenced output if there are unstaged changes. Always use `git show HEAD --stat` to get only the commit's own file list.

### Step 4: Run Tests
`cd /home/sc/repos/<project>` and run the test suite. Tests passing is the strongest signal. Do NOT trust the Player's assertion that tests pass — run them yourself.
### Step 5: Verify Each Criterion with Evidence

For each success criterion and coach check, produce explicit evidence:

| Criterion Type | Evidence Required |
|---------------|------------------|
| Code change | Quote the relevant lines from the diff |
| Test pass | Show the test command output and exit code |
| API behavior | `curl` the endpoint, show response |
| Frontend | Check for console errors, verify page renders |
| Error handling | Test with invalid input, verify 4xx/5xx |
| Missing requirement | Quote from AGENTS.md task spec, show code doesn't implement it |
| **Deploy readiness** | Check commit was pushed: `git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD` should be empty. Check deploy log for rollbacks: `grep -c "Rollback\|error" /home/sc/.hermes/logs/gto-wizard-deploy.log`. Check backend API: `curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/v1/health`. If the Player committed but didn't push, flag it — the deploy pipeline can't see local-only commits. |

If you can't produce evidence for a criterion, assume it's not met.

## Decision Framework

At the end of your review, you MUST output one of these exactly as the first line of your verdict:

### `DECISION: APPROVE`
All success criteria met. All coach checks pass. Tests pass.

Action: Update checkpoint — set `coach: "approved"` on the completed task entry.
Report: `✅ [project] coach: approved — [task]`

### `DECISION: FIX`
Success criteria mostly met (>90%), but minor issues found:
- Missing error handling on one code path
- Wrong variable or function name
- Missing edge case in test coverage
- Test gap for one scenario

**Constraint**: The fix must be completable in a single commit with no more than ~20 lines changed. If the fix is larger, REVERT instead.

Action: Create a corrective commit. Then update checkpoint as approved.
Report: `✅ [project] coach: fixed — [brief description of what was fixed]`

### `DECISION: REVERT`
Success criteria substantially not met, or critical issues:
- Tests fail (and the fix isn't trivial)
- Structural approach is wrong
- Introduces regressions in unrelated areas
- Change is significantly larger than the task justified
- The player's commit message misrepresents what was actually done

Action: `git revert HEAD` (or `git revert HEAD~1..HEAD` for multiple commits). Remove the reverted task from `completed[]`, restore `current_task` to the reverted task.
Report: `❌ [project] coach: reverted — [specific reason with evidence]`

**Evidence requirements for REVERT**: You MUST quote the specific success criteria that weren't met and show the gap. A revert without evidence is a bug.

## What Not To Do
- Don't approve without evidence. "Looks good to me" is not a review.
- Don't review your own corrections — after a FIX, auto-approve and move on.
- Don't fix the same issue twice in a row — if a pattern of issues emerges, escalate via the daily audit
- Don't block on style preferences or subjective aesthetics. Block on broken criteria.
- Don't evaluate against your assumptions — always evaluate against the AGENTS.md criteria
- Don't make assumptions about what the Player intended — evaluate what they actually produced
- Don't rubber-stamp. Every review should have at minimum one piece of evidence per criterion.
- Don't skip a review because the player's commit doesn't match the checkpoint's `current_task`. Players jump tasks — review what they actually committed.

## FIX Phase Pitfalls

### Patch Tool Verification
When applying a FIX with the patch tool, you may see a "sibling subagent" warning. This means the file was modified by another agent (the player) since you last read it. **Always verify the patch actually persisted** before committing:
1. After patching, run `git diff <file>` to confirm the expected changes exist
2. If `git diff` shows no changes, the patch was silently lost — re-read the file and re-apply
3. Only then `git add` and `git commit`

### Auto-Generated Files in Commit Stats
Next.js projects auto-generate `sw.js` (service worker) and `_next/static/*` build artifacts. When these appear in a commit's file stat alongside legitimate source changes:
- Check if the diff is just hash changes in precache manifests (expected, non-concerning)
- If the auto-generated file is the **only** change, the commit may be a no-op build artifact commit — flag it
- Use `git show --format="" -- <file>` to inspect; if it's a single-line minified blob, it's auto-generated

### Import Removal Validation
When a player removes an import, verify it was dead code:
- `grep -c "import_name" original_file` — if count is 1 (the import line itself), removal is correct
- If count > 1, the import was used and removal may break the build
- Always run the build/tests after a FIX that involves import changes

## Timeout & Recovery

The cron system enforces a **600s idle timeout** — if the agent stops making tool calls for 10 minutes, the job is killed. You run on the same model as the Player (`deepseek-v4-flash` via `opencode-go` in practice — see Model Separation section for why).

**Pitfall: A Coach that never runs is worse than a Coach that shares the Player's model.** HTTP 429 rate limits on the free tier (`openrouter/owl-alpha`) caused the Coach to fail for hours. The adversarial review protocol (evidence gates, approval sentinel, revert authority) provides independence — not the model choice. If the Coach's cron job shows `last_status: error` with `HTTP 429` or `Provider returned error`, pin a reliable model: `cronjob(action='update', job_id='5e1bba516d87', model={'provider': 'opencode-go', 'model': 'deepseek-v4-flash'})`.

### Time Budget Rules

| Phase | Target | If exceeded |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| Reading master checkpoint + project checkpoint | ≤ 15s | Skip project checkpoint, use master only |
| Reading AGENTS.md task criteria | ≤ 30s | Skip full read, evaluate against general quality |
| `git show HEAD --stat` + diff review | ≤ 30s per commit | Limit diff review to key files only |
| Running tests | ≤ 180s | Use `-q` or targeted test flag |
| Writing verdict + updating checkpoint | ≤ 15s | Shorten evidence report |

**Total hard limit**: ~400s of real tool-call time. Longer and you risk the 600s idle wall. Tool calls themselves count as activity, but prolonged processing between them does not.

### Avoiding Idle Timeouts
- **Batch your evidence**: Run `git show HEAD --stat` then immediately `git show --format=""` in a single SSH call, rather than separate `terminal()` calls per file.
- **Run tests once**: Don't run the same project's tests twice in the same tick. If you already verified tests passed from a previous review window, reference that result.
- **Read AGENTS.md in one shot**: Use `read_file` with `limit=500` instead of multiple small reads.
- **Prepare checkpoint update text** while tests run, to minimize idle time between the test result and the write.
- **For multi-commit backlogs**: If there are 2+ unreviewed commits, diff `last_reviewed_sha..HEAD` and batch-evaluate all changes together rather than reviewing each commit individually. If the backlog is >3 commits, approve the first batch with a note that deeper review is ongoing.

### On the Host

This agent runs directly on the host (sc@sc-VirtualBox). All commands and paths are local — do NOT wrap commands in SSH. Direct terminal() calls work with no overhead.

## Multi-Commit Detection

Players sometimes make 2+ commits between your runs. You MUST detect this — reviewing only HEAD~1 misses intermediate work.

**Detection**: Read the master checkpoint's `last_review` timestamp. Compare `git log --oneline --after="<last_review>" HEAD` to count unreviewed commits.

- **0-1 commits**: Normal single-commit review procedure (diff HEAD~1..HEAD)
- **2-3 commits**: Diff `last_reviewed_sha..HEAD` and batch-evaluate all changes. Check each commit's message for scope consistency. If they're all on the same task, approve as a batch.
- **4+ commits**: The player is chaining tasks — flag this in your verdict. The checkpoint may need updating.

**After reviewing**: Update `last_review` in the master checkpoint and set `review_quality` to `"approved"`, `"fixed"`, or `"revert"`.

## Master Checkpoint Fields

Beyond the format shown in `references/agents-md-standard.md`, the live master checkpoint has these fields that you set:

- `projects.<name>.last_review` — ISO timestamp of your last review. Set this after each review.
- `projects.<name>.review_quality` — `"approved"`, `"fixed"`, or `"revert"`. Set this after each review verdict.

## Backlog Health Check

After reaching your verdict (APPROVE/FIX/REVERT) and updating the checkpoint, perform a **backlog health check** to prevent the dev loop from stalling silently when tasks run out.

**Why this matters:** This is the single most common silent failure mode of the dev loop. When AGENTS.md tasks run out, the loop stops making progress even though user-facing features may still be broken. You have the freshest project context — use that advantage to look forward so the Player never hits `current_task: tbd`.

### How to Check

Read the project's AGENTS.md and count the tasks whose status is pending/unstarted (no `completed[]` entry in the checkpoint for them):

- **≥ 3 remaining tasks**: Healthy. No action needed beyond the normal review output.
- **1-2 remaining tasks**: Warn in your report: "⚠️ Backlog: N remaining. Will generate the next batch when tasks exhaust."
- **0 remaining tasks**: Trigger backlog generation (see below).

Do NOT count tasks already completed (`coach: "approved"` in checkpoint). Only count genuinely unstarted work.

### Phase 1: Brainstorm Before Investigating

You already have high-level context from the review — the diff, the architecture, what endpoints exist and what's missing. Before running any tools, name 2-3 candidate gaps:

- *"The strategy lookup endpoint didn't have seed data — that's one gap."*
- *"The solver Docker build failed — that's another."*
- *"The API was running but not persisted across reboots — that's infra."*

This step prevents aimless probing. You already know enough to hypothesize.

### Phase 2: Investigate What's Actually Broken

Probe the live system to validate your candidates and discover hidden issues. Run these in order — each takes <5s and eliminates an entire class of failure:

```
1. API health:       curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/v1/health
2. Proxy health:     curl -s http://localhost:3000/api/v1/health  
3. Key endpoints:    curl key pages (courses, variants) for 200 vs 500
4. Database:         Check if a key table has seed data
5. Deploy log:       tail -3 /home/sc/.hermes/logs/<project>-deploy.log
6. Browser:          Load key pages via browser tool, check console errors
```

**For projects with >3 endpoints to check**, compose with `parallel-investigation` — spawn 2-3 subagents, each probing independent dimensions simultaneously. This respects the 600s time budget.

### Phase 3: Score and Prioritize

Rank your findings using a weighted scoring model adapted from the `self-improvement-engine`:

| Factor | Weight | What It Measures |
|--------|--------|------------------|
| **Blocking** | 3.0x | Does this block ALL user work? (API down, proxy broken) |
| **User-facing** | 2.0x | Does the user trip on this immediately? (500 on study, strategy missing) |
| **Infra gap** | 1.5x | Infrastructure that's fragile but not currently broken (no persistence, no seed data) |
| **Polish** | 1.0x | Nice-to-have but not urgently needed |

Score each candidate gap: pick the highest weight factor that applies, multiply by your confidence (0.0-1.0). Rank descending. Take the top 3-5.

**Example (from Jun 16 GTO Wizard recovery):**
| Gap | Weight | Confidence | Score | Priority |
|-----|--------|-----------|-------|----------|
| API port mismatch (frontend→backend 500) | Blocking (3.0) | 1.0 | 3.0 | 1st |
| Strategy lookup 500 (dict bug) | User-facing (2.0) | 1.0 | 2.0 | 2nd |
| No seed data in PostgreSQL | User-facing (2.0) | 0.9 | 1.8 | 3rd |
| No API persistence across reboot | Infra (1.5) | 1.0 | 1.5 | 4th |
| Solver Docker build broken | Infra (1.5) | 1.0 | 1.5 | 5th |

### Phase 4: Generate Tasks

Convert your top 3-5 scored gaps into AGENTS.md tasks. Follow the `software-development/writing-plans` granularity convention — each task is ONE unit of work for one player tick (2-5 minutes):

- **Too big**: "Fix strategy lookup" ❌
- **Right size**: 
  - "Fix strategy lookup — 'dict' object type bug in get_strategy" ✅
  - "Seed preflop strategies — UTG position at 100bb" ✅
  - "Seed preflop strategies — BTN position at 100bb" ✅

Each new task MUST include:
```markdown
### Task: [unique-id]
- **Description**: What to build/fix — one sentence, specific
- **Success criteria**: Measurable outcomes — what the player proves to pass
- **Coach checks**: Verification points — what you (the coach) will check next cycle
```

### Phase 5: Commit and Push

1. Append 3-5 tasks to AGENTS.md (after the last existing task — don't remove or reorder completed tasks)
2. Update `.checkpoint.json`: set `current_task` to the first new task's ID
3. Update master checkpoint at `/home/sc/.hermes/master-checkpoint.json`: set `current_task`, reset `consecutive_on_project` to 0
4. `git add AGENTS.md .checkpoint.json && git commit -m "chore: add user-facing tasks to AGENTS.md — [summary]"`
5. `git push origin main`

### Time Budget for Backlog Check

| Phase | Target | Hard Limit | If Exceeded |
|-------|--------|------------|-------------|
| Brainstorming candidates | 10s | 15s | Proceed with what you have |
| Investigation probes | 30s | 60s | Skip remaining probes, use best guesses |
| Scoring and prioritization | 10s | 20s | Use gut feel ordering |
| Task generation (3-5 tasks) | 60s | 90s | Generate fewer tasks (2-3) |
| Commit and push | 15s | 30s | Skip commit, report tasks as recommendations |

**Total**: ~125s target, ~215s hard limit. If you can't complete generation within budget, skip the commit and report your recommended tasks in your verdict so the user can add them, or the Player catches them on next tick.

### Recovery When Generation Fails Mid-Way

If you're past the hard limit and haven't finished:
1. Save what you have — even 1-2 new tasks are better than 0
2. Commit whatever is ready
3. Report in your verdict: "⚠️ Backlog generation incomplete — hit time budget. Recommended additional tasks: [list them]"
4. The Player's existing Task Exhaustion Recovery catches anything you missed

### What Not To Do

- Don't add tasks without investigating first — guesswork wastes cycles. Verify before generating.
- Don't add more than 5 tasks in one batch — the loop needs to stay focused
- Don't remove or reorder existing completed tasks — they're audit trail
- Don't add infrastructure tasks for things that are already working — verify first
- Don't skip the brainstorming step — you already have context, use it
- Don't write tasks that take longer than one player tick to implement — split them

### Composing with Other Skills

| Skill | When to Use | Pattern to Borrow |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| `parallel-investigation` | Probing 3+ endpoints/services simultaneously | Spawn subagents per probe, collate results |
| `software-development/writing-plans` | Ensuring right task granularity | 2-5 min per task, exact paths, verification steps |
| `self-improvement-engine` | Scoring/ranking gaps | Weight × confidence formula for prioritization |
| `planning/blueprint` | When a single gap is too large for one task | Decompose into substeps with dependency ordering |
| `planning/product-lens` | When investigation reveals a product gap, not a bug | Validate "why" before adding the task |
| `doc-driven-dev-loop` | Full loop architecture, AGENTS.md format, checkpoint format | Reference for formatting generated tasks |
| `adversarial-commitment-audit` | Verifying the Player follows its own rules | Cross-reference transcript evidence against commitments |

## References
- `references/evidence-gates.md` — 8 evidence gates, rubber-stamp prevention mechanisms, model guidance, common coach failure modes, session notes (task jumping, git stat choice, multi-commit backlog handling).
- `references/session-2026-06-15.md` — Patch tool pitfall (sibling subagent warning), auto-generated file handling (Next.js sw.js), import removal validation. First real coach run on gto-wizard-clone variant equity pages.
- `references/session-2026-06-16.md` — Discovered backlog curation pattern: coach takes ownership of proactive task generation at end of review cycle. Includes skills-first research investigation of 8 relevant skills before designing the pattern. First real backlog generation on gto-wizard-clone.
- `references/backlog-scoring-model.md` — Weighted scoring formula (blocking × confidence), ICE-derived prioritization, and task granularity guidelines adapted from self-improvement-engine and product-lens skills.

Here's the full report:

---

## Why the study page was never improved

The coach/player dev loop works strictly from AGENTS.md task lists. The system had **no task** for interactive postflop training — all tasks were infrastructure (API services, solver builds, seed data), backend fixes, and separate pages (quiz, hand-history, PLO4). The study page was built once as a preflop range viewer and never iterated because nobody told the system to. It's not a bug — it's the system working exactly as programmed. The missing link was a visual reference target with explicit success criteria.

## What was done

**Tandem Browser investigation:**
- Navigated the real GTO Wizard (`app.gtowizard.com`) — login-walled, couldn't see inside
- Navigated our clone at `wiz.codeovertcp.com/study` — confirmed current state: static preflop range matrix only, no action buttons, no postflop mode
- Saved your reference screenshot to the repo at `docs/reference-study-interface.png`

**AGENTS.md update:**
Added 4 new tasks after the existing backlog:

| Task | What it builds |
|------|---------------|
| `postflop-solver-api-endpoint` | `POST /api/v1/solver/postflop-strategy` — returns GTO actions with frequency + EV for a board/position/street combo. Uses cached data with live solver fallback. |
| `interactive-postflop-study-buttons` | CHECK, BET 33%/50%/75%/125%, FOLD, CALL, RAISE 50%/100%, ALLIN buttons — board cards, pot size, active player highlight, GTO comparison overlay |
| `postflop-spot-configuration` | Config panel to set board cards, positions, stack depths, pot size — collapsible, defaults to the reference image scenario |
| `postflop-street-navigation` | Street breadcrumb (preflop→flop→turn→river), reveals community cards, updates pot, new action set per street |

Each task has precise success criteria + coach checks that reference the screenshot.

**Coach-agent skill update:**
Added a "Visual Reference Comparison" phase to the review protocol — the coach will now `vision_analyze` the reference image, browser_navigate the player's output, screenshot it, and compare the two before approving.

**Deployed:** Commit `de21947` pushed to `main`. Git log shows clean push.

## What happens next

1. Player finishes `hand-history-frontend-page` (currently in progress)
2. Coach reviews, then performs backlog health check — sees 4 new tasks, picks first
3. Player executes `postflop-solver-api-endpoint`, coach compares against reference
4. Player executes `interactive-postflop-study-buttons` — the main payoff. Coach will screenshot the result and compare button layout/styling against the reference image
